concepts.title: pH scale
concepts.conceptBody:
pH SCALE:

$\text{pH}$ of solution is the negative logarithm to the base 10 of the concentration of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions which it contains (Basically P acts as a mathematical operator: $p(A)=-{{\log }_{10}}[~\text{A}]$)

\[\Rightarrow \] \[\mathbf{pH=}\mathbf{lo}{{\mathbf{g}}_{\mathbf{10}}}\mathbf{[}{{\mathbf{H}}^{\mathbf{+}}}\mathbf{]}\]

At $25\text{C}$, using this definition, a scale called $\text{pH}$ scale is developed as follows:

For ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O},{{\text{H}}^{+}}={{10}^{-7}}\text{M}$

$\text{pH}\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)=-\log \left( {{10}^{-7}} \right)=7$

$\Rightarrow \quad $ For a neutral solution; $\text{pH}=7$

(1) If $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]>{{10}^{-7}}$ in a solution i.e., solution is acidic then $\text{pH}<7$

(2) If $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]<{{10}^{-7}}\left( {} \right.$ or $\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]>{{10}^{-7}}$) i.e. solution is basic then $\text{pH}>7$.

Acidic \[{{H}_{2}}O\] Basic

At 25°C 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 10 11

(298 K)

\[\text{Acidity increases }\xleftarrow[{}]{{}}\underset{(\text{Neutral})}{\mathop{\text{ph}}}\,\xrightarrow[{}]{{}}\text{Basicity increases}\]

concepts.title: pH calculation of strong acid
concepts.conceptBody:
1. pH Calculation of strong acid

A strong acid completely ionises in water.

For example, $\text{HCl}\to {{\text{H}}^{+}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

Let $\text{C}$ be the concentration of $\text{HCl}$.

The concentration of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$obtained from $\text{HCl}$ will also be $\text{C}$.

$\therefore \text{pH}=-\log \text{C}$

This process cannot be used to calculate the $\text{pH}$ of ${{10}^{-7}}\text{MHCl}$. Since, we know that any acidic solution will have $\text{pH}$ less than 7 , we must adopt some other method.

Method I

We already know the concentration of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$(or ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$) ions from pure water is ${{10}^{-7}}\text{M}$. So all we need to do is to add the ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ion concentration from water and ${{\text{H}}^{+}}\left( {} \right.$or ${{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$) ion concentration from $\text{HCl}$ to get the ${{\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]}_{\text{Total }}}$.

$\therefore \quad {{\left( {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right)}_{\text{T}}}={{\left( {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right)}_{\text{HCl}}}+{{\left( {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right)}_{\text{water }}}={{10}^{-7}}\text{M}+{{10}^{-7}}\text{M}=2\times {{10}^{-7}}\text{M}$

$\therefore \quad \text{pH}=-\log \left( {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right)=-\log 2\quad {{10}^{-7}}=6.6989$

Though the method looks correct but there is a mistake in it. In chemical equilibrium we have learnt about Le-ChatelierÊs Principle, which states that when concentration of a reactant or product is changed the reaction tends of go forward or reverse to finally reestablish equilibrium. We can see that in the present case, by adding \[{{10}^{-7}}\,M\,\,HCl\] to water, we are increasing the $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$. This should make the reaction go in the reverse direction. Therefore the $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$from water would become less than ${{10}^{-7}}\text{M}$. So the method will yield incorrect result.

Method II

The statement given above that "the addition of an ion to an equilibrium, having the same ion makes the equilibrium move in a direction to consume that ion" is called the common ion effect. This implies that water would dissociate less in the presence of $\text{HCl}$. Let the amount of water dissociated be ${{\text{x}}^{\prime }}$ in the presence of ${{10}^{-7}}\text{MHCl}$.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}

2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \\

\left( {{\text{x}}^{\prime }}+{{10}^{-7}} \right)\quad {{\text{x}}^{\prime }} \\

\end{array}$

At equilibrium

$\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]={{10}^{-14}}$

$\left( {{\text{x}}^{\prime }}+{{10}^{-7}} \right)\left( {{\text{x}}^{\prime }} \right)={{10}^{-14}}$

Calculating for ${{x}^{\prime }}$, we get ${{x}^{\prime }}=0.618\quad {{10}^{-7}}\text{M}$.

Here we can clearly see the common ion effect in operation. Water which was dissociating to give ${{10}^{-7}}\text{M}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$ions, has now experienced the common ion effect and has now yielded only $0.618\quad {{10}^{-7}}\text{M}{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$ions.

Therefore, ${{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]}_{\text{T}}}=\left( {{10}^{-7}}+{{0.61810}^{-7}} \right)\text{M}$

$\therefore \quad \text{pH}=-\log \left( 1.618\quad {{10}^{-7}} \right)=\mathbf{6}.\mathbf{7910}$

Now, let us answer the question as to why we take the $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$from water into account for calculating the $\text{pH}$ of ${{10}^{-7}}\text{MHCl}$ while for calculating the $\text{pH}$ of ${{10}^{-6}}\text{M},{{10}^{-5}}\text{M},{{10}^{-4}}\text{M},{{10}^{-3}}\text{M},{{10}^{-2}}\text{M}$ and ${{10}^{-1}}\text{MHCl}$, we have not considered it. It can be seen that the ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ions from water, decreases due to the common ion from $\text{HCl}$. Greater the concentration of the common ion added, greater will be the effect. Therefore, for concentrations higher than ${{10}^{-6}}\text{M}$ (and inclusive of ${{10}^{-6}}\text{M}$), the ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$ from water will be even less than $0.618\quad {{10}^{-7}}\text{M}$ and would be so small in comparison to the $\left[ {{\text{H}}^{+}} \right]$ from $\text{HCl}$, that we can ignore it.

Thus, it is concluded that $\left[ {{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{O}}^{\mathbf{+}}} \right]$from water needs to be considered only if the $\left[ {{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{O}}^{\mathbf{+}}} \right]$ from acid is $\mathbf{<1}{{\mathbf{0}}^{\mathbf{6}}}\mathbf{M}$ and if $\left[ {{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{O}}^{\mathbf{+}}} \right]$from acid is $\le \mathbf{1}{{\mathbf{0}}^{\mathbf{6}}}\mathbf{M}$, the $\left[ {{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{3}}}{{\mathbf{O}}^{\mathbf{+}}} \right]$from water can be ignored.

concepts.title: pH calculation of weak acid
concepts.conceptBody:
2. pH Calculation of Weak Acid

Weak acid is that which dissociate partially in water and soon comes in equilibrium with its ions as

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

The equilibrium constant is called ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ (dissociation or ionization constant).

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}=\frac{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}} \right]\times \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]}{\left[ \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH} \right]}$

Let the degree of dissociation of weak acid be$\alpha \text{. }$

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\rightleftharpoons \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO}{{\text{H}}^{-}}+{{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}}$

Initial conc. c 0 0

Conc. at equilib. $c-c\alpha $ $c\alpha $ $c\alpha $

If $\alpha <<1\text{ than }1-\alpha \approx 1\text{ (i}\text{.e}\text{. for }\alpha \text{ less than or equal to }0.1\text{ ) }$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\approx \text{c}{{\alpha }^{2}}\quad \text{ or }\quad \alpha =\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{\text{c}}}$

$\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=\text{c}\alpha =\text{c}\times \sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}}{\text{c}}}=\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\times \text{c}}$

Using the value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ and $\text{c},\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]$can be calculated and then $\text{pH}$ of the solution can be calculated.

Taking $\log $ of both the sides of above equation,

$\log \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=1/2\log {{\text{K}}_{2}}+1/2\log \text{c}$

$-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=-1/2\log {{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}-1/2\log \text{c}$

$$

concepts.title: Important pH results
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Important Points:

  • Relative strength of two weak acids is a measure of $\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]$

$\frac{{{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]}_{1}}}{{{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}}{~{{\text{K}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}}}$

  • pH of a weak monobasic acid

$\text{pH}=-\log \left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=-\log \sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}\text{C}}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \text{pK}{{\text{a}}_{\text{a}}}-\log \text{C} \right]$

Total $\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]$in a mixture of two weak acids.

$\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]=\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}+{{\text{K}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}}$

  • For weak bases

$\text{BOH}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{B}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$

${{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{B}}^{+}} \right]\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{BOH}]}=\frac{\text{C}{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{(1-\text{x})}=\text{C}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$

$\text{x}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}}{\text{C}}}$

${{\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}_{\text{weak base }}}=\text{Cx}=\sqrt{{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}\text{C}}$

  • Relative strength of two weak bases

$\frac{{{\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}_{1}}}{{{\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]}_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{{{\text{K}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}}{~{{\text{K}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}}}$

  • $\text{pOH}$ of weak monoacid base

$\text{pOH}=-\log \left( \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right)=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{b}}}-\log \text{C} \right]$

$\text{pH}=14-\text{pOH}$

$=14-\frac{1}{2}\left[ p{{K}_{b}}-\log C \right]$

Total $\left[ \text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}} \right]$in a mixture of two weak bases.

$$
